summary: By 1921 Greenwood Avenue anchored roughly 35 square blocks of Black-owned groceries, hotels, theaters, newspapers, and law and medical offices — the 'Black Wall Street' of the nation. Residents rebuilt after the district's 1921 destruction, and by the 1940s Greenwood again supported more than 200 Black-owned businesses.

claims.object: Black Wall Street — a nationally renowned, self-contained Black entrepreneurial district along Greenwood Avenue with nightclubs, hotels, cafes, newspapers, clothiers, theaters, and professional offices
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia of Oklahoma History and Culture: Greenwood District

claims.object: Tulsa Race Massacre of May 31 – June 1, 1921, which left the Greenwood area in ruins; residents subsequently rebuilt the district
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: John Hope Franklin Reconciliation Park

historicalContext: Segregation-era Tulsa concentrated Black commerce north of the Frisco tracks, where entrepreneurs built a self-contained economy that became the most famous Black business district in America. Greenwood's prosperity, destruction, and rebuilding made it the touchstone for the broader story of Black district-building.

summary: By the 1920s Auburn Avenue held Atlanta Life Insurance (founded 1905 by Alonzo Herndon), the 1912 Odd Fellows Building, Big Bethel AME, and the Atlanta Daily World (1928). Civic leader John Wesley Dobbs coined the name 'Sweet Auburn' for its prosperity; the district was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1976.

claims.object: Sweet Auburn Historic District, designated by the Secretary of the Interior in 1976 for its late-19th to early-20th century Black commercial, residential, and religious buildings
claims.citationLabel: NPS Heritage Documentation Programs: Sweet Auburn Historic District

claims.object: Atlanta's African American commercial, social, religious, and political life in the early twentieth century, and the neighborhood where Martin Luther King Jr. was born in 1929
claims.citationLabel: Martin Luther King, Jr. National Historical Park: The Sweet Auburn Community

historicalContext: After the 1906 Atlanta race riot pushed Black business off Peachtree Street, Auburn Avenue became the city's concentrated corridor of Black banking, insurance, publishing, and church life — one of the wealthiest Black streets in America by the interwar years.

summary: Chicago's 'Black Metropolis' grew with the Great Migration after 1916, centered on State Street and South Parkway: the Chicago Defender, Overton Hygienic Building (1922-23), Chicago Bee Building, Supreme Life Insurance, and the 1913 Wabash Avenue YMCA. Chicago landmarked the Black Metropolis-Bronzeville District in 1998.

claims.object: Black Metropolis-Bronzeville District, designated a Chicago Landmark district in 1998, comprising eight historic buildings and the Victory Monument
claims.citationLabel: City of Chicago Historic Preservation: Black Metropolis-Bronzeville District

claims.object: Bronzeville-Black Metropolis National Heritage Area, designated by Congress in 2023, covering more than 200 sites between 18th and 71st Streets
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Bronzeville-Black Metropolis National Heritage Area

historicalContext: The Great Migration concentrated hundreds of thousands of Black Southerners on Chicago's South Side, where a parallel economy of banks, insurance firms, publishers, and entertainment venues earned Bronzeville its 'Black Metropolis' name in the 1945 study by St. Clair Drake and Horace Cayton.

summary: After Philip A. Payton Jr.'s Afro-American Realty Company began opening Harlem housing to Black tenants in 1904, the neighborhood went from roughly 10 percent African American in 1910 to about 70 percent by 1930, becoming the capital of the Harlem Renaissance and home to institutions like the Apollo Theater and the Schomburg collection.

claims.object: Approximately 70 percent African American by 1930, up from approximately 10 percent in 1910, as the neighborhood became the center of the Harlem Renaissance
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Harlem

claims.object: The Apollo Theater, listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1983 as the nation's premier performance hall for Black American performers
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Apollo Theater

historicalContext: Overbuilt speculative housing and Black-run realty companies opened uptown Manhattan to Black New Yorkers and Southern migrants, and by the 1920s Harlem was the largest and most influential Black urban community in the country — the stage for the literary, musical, and political flowering of the Harlem Renaissance.

summary: From the 1890s Beale Street was the main street of Black Memphis: Robert Church Sr.'s Church Park and Auditorium opened in 1899, Ida B. Wells edited the Free Speech nearby, and W. C. Handy published 'The Memphis Blues' in 1912. The street's blues legacy earned it National Historic Landmark designation in 1966.

claims.object: Beale Street Historic District, listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1966 (reference 66000731) for its significance in the history of the blues
claims.citationLabel: National Register of Historic Places #66000731: Beale Street Historic District

claims.object: Beale Street Historic District marker noting the street's designation as a Registered National Historic Landmark
claims.citationLabel: Historical Marker Database: Beale Street Historic District

historicalContext: Beale Street concentrated Black commerce, churches, fraternal halls, and entertainment in postbellum Memphis, and its saloons and theaters incubated the urban blues that W. C. Handy first set to paper — making it the model of the Black main street as cultural engine.

summary: Between the 1890s and 1920s, U Street NW became Washington's 'Black Broadway' — the True Reformer Building (1903), Howard Theatre (1910), the nation's first full-service Black YMCA, and the Whitelaw Hotel among hundreds of Black-owned businesses. The historic district, designated in 1998, has a period of significance of 1862-1948.

claims.object: Greater U Street Historic District, designated in 1998 with a period of significance of 1862-1948
claims.citationLabel: DC Office of Planning, Historic Preservation Office: U Street Historic District

claims.object: Washington's 'Black Broadway' during segregation — the heart of African American business and culture, with hundreds of Black-owned businesses, banks, churches, and theaters by the 1920s
claims.citationLabel: Cultural Tourism DC: Greater U Street Heritage Trail

historicalContext: Home to Howard University and a large educated Black middle class, segregated Washington channeled Black enterprise and entertainment into the U Street corridor, which rivaled Harlem as a center of Black theater, jazz, and professional life before 1948.

summary: Founded by freedpeople after the Civil War south of Durham's rail line, Hayti grew commercial spines along Fayetteville, Pettigrew, and Pine Streets, anchored by St. Joseph's AME Church (1891) and Lincoln Hospital (1901). Booker T. Washington praised the community, whose builders also created N.C. Mutual, the nation's largest Black-owned insurance company.

claims.object: St. Joseph's AME Church, built 1891, the last remaining original structure of Durham's historic Black district, preserved since 1975 as the Hayti Heritage Center
claims.citationLabel: St. Joseph's Historic Foundation / Hayti Heritage Center

claims.object: A self-sufficient African American community founded by freedpeople, later disrupted when 1960s urban renewal and the Durham Freeway displaced thousands of homes and hundreds of Black-owned businesses
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Civil Rights Trail: Hayti Heritage Center

historicalContext: Durham's tobacco economy and a cohort of Black entrepreneurs made Hayti and nearby Parrish Street a national exemplar of Black economic self-determination, studied and praised by both Booker T. Washington and W. E. B. Du Bois in the early 1900s.

summary: By 1900 Richmond's Jackson Ward was the 'Cradle of Black Capitalism': the True Reformers' bank (chartered 1888) and Maggie L. Walker's St. Luke Penny Savings Bank (1903) — she was the first American woman to charter a bank — grew among fraternal orders and insurance firms. The 42-block district became a National Historic Landmark in 1978.

claims.object: Jackson Ward Historic District, listed in the National Register in 1976 and designated a National Historic Landmark in 1978, one of the largest landmark districts associated with African American history
claims.citationLabel: Virginia Department of Historic Resources: Jackson Ward Historic District (127-0237)

claims.object: One of the nation's most thriving Black business communities at the turn of the 20th century, with Black-founded banks, insurance companies, and fraternal organizations, including Maggie L. Walker's St. Luke Penny Savings Bank
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Jackson Ward Walking Tour

historicalContext: Antebellum free Black Richmonders and postwar freedpeople built Jackson Ward's dense fabric of churches and fraternal societies into a financial center, proving Black-run banking and insurance viable a generation after emancipation.

summary: Subdivided in the 1790s from Claude Tremé's plantation just outside the French Quarter, Faubourg Tremé is among the oldest Black neighborhoods in the United States: free people of color owned property here before the Civil War, St. Augustine Catholic Church opened in 1841, and the gatherings at nearby Congo Square fed directly into the birth of jazz.

claims.object: Tremé local historic district regulated by the New Orleans Historic District Landmarks Commission, encompassing the early-19th-century faubourg where free people of color owned and built property
claims.citationLabel: City of New Orleans HDLC: Tremé Historic District

claims.object: Early jazz culture — brass band, church, and benevolent-society traditions in the neighborhoods around Congo Square shaped the emergence of jazz
claims.citationLabel: New Orleans Jazz National Historical Park: Jazz Neighborhoods

historicalContext: New Orleans's uniquely large antebellum population of free people of color made Tremé a center of Black property ownership, Catholic parish life, and mutual-aid societies decades before emancipation — a continuity of institutions that later carried brass-band and Mardi Gras traditions into jazz.

summary: From the 1890s through the 1960s the 125-acre Farish Street district was the largest economically independent Black community in Mississippi — Black-owned banks, drugstores, funeral homes, churches, the Alamo Theatre, and record labels that cut Delta blues sides. The district was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1980.

claims.object: Farish Street Neighborhood Historic District, a 125-acre district listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1980
claims.citationLabel: Mississippi Department of Archives and History: The Rise and Fall of Farish Street

claims.object: The largest economically independent African American community in Mississippi through the 1960s, a business, social, cultural, and residential center from the 1880s onward
claims.citationLabel: Visit Jackson: Farish Street Historic District

historicalContext: In the capital of the most rigidly segregated Southern state, Farish Street's professionals, undertakers, publishers, and musicians built a parallel main street that sustained Black civic life — and sheltered civil rights organizing — for three generations.

summary: After 1900, Jim Crow ordinances pushed Birmingham's growing Black business community into the blocks along Third, Fourth, and Fifth Avenues North between 15th and 18th Streets. At its peak the district held more than 300 Black-owned businesses around the Colored Masonic Temple; it was listed in the National Register in 1982 and remains majority Black-owned.

claims.object: Fourth Avenue Historic District, listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1982
claims.citationLabel: Historical Marker Database: Fourth Avenue Historic District

claims.object: More than 300 Black-owned businesses at the district's height, anchored by the Masonic Temple with its library, soda fountain, and ballroom that hosted Duke Ellington and Count Basie
claims.citationLabel: Birmingham Civil Rights District: Fourth Avenue Historic District

historicalContext: Industrial Birmingham's Black workforce, barred from white commerce, built a compact downtown district of theaters, cafes, and professional offices — one of the few Southern Black business districts still operating today with majority Black ownership.

summary: By the 1920s the 300 block of Northeast Second Street — 'Deep Deuce' — was Oklahoma City's Black main street: barbershops, doctors' and lawyers' offices, a movie theater, and Roscoe Dunjee's Black Dispatch newspaper at 324 NE Second, with dance halls where Jimmy Rushing and Charlie Christian began national careers.

claims.object: Oklahoma City's African American business district by day and cultural center by night from the early 1900s, known by the 1920s as Deep Deuce, Deep Two, and Deep Second
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia of Oklahoma History and Culture: Second Street

claims.object: Roscoe Dunjee, editor of the Black Dispatch, who used the press, courts, and legislature to fight segregation in housing, education, and public facilities
claims.citationLabel: Historical Marker Database: Roscoe Dunjee

historicalContext: Segregated Oklahoma City compressed Black commerce and nightlife into a few blocks of Second Street, whose jazz scene — Jimmy Rushing, Charlie Christian, the Blue Devils orbit — projected a small district onto the national stage.

summary: Incorporated on August 15, 1887, by 27 registered Black voters, Eatonville is the oldest incorporated African American municipality in the United States. The Hungerford Normal and Industrial School opened in 1897, and the town shaped the work of Zora Neale Hurston, who grew up here and set much of her writing in Eatonville.

claims.object: 1887 — the first incorporated African American community in America, with a preservation trail of ten historic sites
claims.citationLabel: Historical Marker Database: Eatonville, Florida

claims.object: 'The town that freedom built' — the oldest incorporated African American municipality in America, as proclaimed on the town's gateway arch
claims.citationLabel: Historical Marker Database: Eatonville Gateway

historicalContext: Eatonville led the wave of self-governing all-Black towns founded after Reconstruction, proving Black municipal self-rule a generation before Oklahoma's town-building boom — and giving American literature the setting Zora Neale Hurston made famous.

summary: Founded July 12, 1887, in the Yazoo Delta by Isaiah T. Montgomery and Benjamin T. Green, formerly enslaved at Davis Bend, and incorporated in 1898, Mound Bayou became the nation's leading self-governing all-Black town, with private businesses, a high school, and two hospitals serving Black Mississippians across the Delta.

claims.object: 1887, by cousins Isaiah T. Montgomery and Benjamin T. Green, formerly enslaved at the Davis Bend plantation, on land purchased from the Louisville, New Orleans and Texas Railroad
claims.citationLabel: City of Mound Bayou: City History

claims.object: Mississippi Department of Archives and History marker (2017) recording the town's 1887 founding, 1898 incorporation, and its schools and two hospitals serving the Delta
claims.citationLabel: Historical Marker Database: Mound Bayou

historicalContext: Mound Bayou was the flagship of post-Reconstruction Black town-building in the Deep South — a cooperative, self-governed refuge whose farms, bank, and Taborian Hospital demonstrated Black institutional capacity inside the most repressive corner of the Jim Crow order.

summary: Founded in 1877 by Black settlers from Kentucky on the high plains of Graham County, Nicodemus is the oldest surviving Black homesteader town west of the Mississippi. By 1880 it had a bank, two hotels, three churches, and general stores; five surviving buildings are preserved as Nicodemus National Historic Site, established in 1996.

claims.object: Nicodemus National Historic Site, a unit of the National Park System preserving the town's five surviving historic buildings
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Nicodemus National Historic Site

claims.object: 1877, by the Nicodemus Town Company organized April 18, 1877, with W. H. Smith as president; roughly 350 settlers from Kentucky arrived in September 1877
claims.citationLabel: Kansas Historical Society, Kansapedia: Nicodemus, Graham County

historicalContext: Nicodemus preceded and helped inspire the Exoduster migration of 1879-1881, when tens of thousands of Black Southerners moved to Kansas seeking land and self-government — the first great westward Black town-building movement after Reconstruction.

summary: Founded after 1865 at Magazine Point, north of Mobile, by West Africans trafficked aboard the schooner Clotilda in 1860, Africatown's founders bought land collectively, elected their own leaders, and established Union Missionary Baptist Church and a school while keeping African language and custom. The historic district was listed in the National Register in 2012.

claims.object: Freed survivors of the schooner Clotilda, the last documented U.S. slave ship (1860), who settled Magazine Point after the Civil War, elected their own officials, purchased land collectively, and established their own church
claims.citationLabel: Historical Marker Database: Africatown

claims.object: Africatown Heritage House, the Mobile County museum housing 'Clotilda: The Exhibition' on the ship's 2019 rediscovery and the community's history
claims.citationLabel: Mobile County: Africatown Heritage House

historicalContext: Africatown is the only known U.S. community founded and governed by African-born survivors of the transatlantic slave trade — a direct transplant of West African social organization onto American soil, sustained through family lines that remain in the neighborhood today.

summary: Begun in 1838 when James Weeks, a free Black stevedore, purchased land in central Brooklyn, Weeksville grew into one of antebellum America's largest free Black communities, with churches, schools, an orphan asylum, and its own newspapers. The surviving Hunterfly Road Houses (c. 1840-1880) were designated New York City landmarks in 1970.

claims.object: Houses on Hunterfly Road, 1698-1708 Bergen Street, designated New York City Landmarks in 1970 as surviving structures of the Weeksville community
claims.citationLabel: NYC Landmarks Preservation Commission: Houses on Hunterfly Road designation report

claims.object: One of the nation's oldest and largest free Black communities before the Civil War, whose four surviving wood-frame houses were restored by the City of New York in a project completed in 2026
claims.citationLabel: NYC Department of Design and Construction: Weeksville Hunterfly Road Houses restoration

historicalContext: Founded a decade after New York's 1827 abolition of slavery, Weeksville let Black men meet property qualifications for voting and built independent institutions — a rare, well-documented example of antebellum Northern Black community-building on its original site.

summary: Founded in 1908 in Tulare County by Colonel Allen Allensworth — born enslaved, retired as the Army's highest-ranking Black chaplain — with William Payne and three partners, Allensworth was the only California town founded, financed, and governed by African Americans, with a school, library, church, hotel, and businesses on an 800-acre site.

claims.object: 1908, by Lt. Colonel Allen Allensworth, Professor William Payne, and three partners — the only California town founded, financed, and governed by African Americans
claims.citationLabel: California State Parks: Colonel Allensworth State Historic Park

claims.object: Colonel Allensworth State Historic Park, established in 1976, with restored buildings including the schoolhouse, hotel, general store, and library
claims.citationLabel: Friends of Allensworth

historicalContext: Allensworth carried the all-Black town movement to the Far West, pairing Booker T. Washington-style self-help with California farmland — and though water shortfalls stalled its growth, the restored townsite now anchors the state's premier site of Black pioneer history.
